state.organisation: Istanbul Kültür University, Computer Engineering Dept.
state.targetPopulation: SEN4012 Analysis of Algorithms students (3rd/4th year, n≈35)
state.problemStatement: Students understand sorting algorithm names but cannot trace execution on a given array. SortQuest makes sorting visual and tactile: drag tiles into correct positions following real algorithm rules. Wrong individual moves are rejected immediately with an algorithm-specific explanation — there is no path to a correct final state through random dragging.
state.deliverableArea: Algorithm Design, Complexity Analysis, Problem-Solving Skills, Computational Thinking, Practical Coding Application

frs.desc: CSS Grid tile drag-and-drop; colour-coded by relative value
Validation rejects every invalid drag move with algorithm-specific tooltip
8 puzzle modes each with distinct validation function and tile layout
Star rating (1–3): moves vs min, hints, time vs par
Daily Sort Challenge: global seed, no-hint, leaderboard by moves + time
Level Editor: array input + mode + shareable URL

nfrs.desc: Validation rejects 100% of invalid moves all 8 modes
nfrs.target: 200 invalid scenarios per mode, 0 false accepts (automated test suite)

userStories.want: drag numbered tiles following algorithm rules
userStories.soThat: I practise execution in a tactile visual way
userStories.acc: Valid moves snap + animate; invalid = red flash + tooltip with violated rule; move counter updates

userStories.want: see star rating after each puzzle
userStories.soThat: I know how efficient my execution was
userStories.acc: 1–3 stars; breakdown: moves vs min, hints, time vs par; shown before leaderboard

userStories.want: compete in a daily global puzzle with all classmates
userStories.soThat: I can fairly compare execution skill
userStories.acc: Daily tab; seed + expiry shown; no hints; leaderboard top 10; results after 24h

userStories.want: create a custom puzzle and share it as a URL
userStories.acc: Level Editor; array + mode → shareable URL; opens in play mode for anyone

sixDim.what: 8 puzzle modes (Sequential Sort, Binary Search, Insertion Sort, MergeSort, QuickSort, HeapSort, DP Knapsack, BFS Graph); drag-and-drop tile engine with algorithm-rule validation; star rating (★★★ = min moves + under par time); hint system (−1 star); Daily Sort Challenge (global seed, leaderboard); Level Editor (shareable URL)
sixDim.why: Flow: 4 tiles (level 1) → 64 tiles (level 5). SDT Competence: visible improvement. MDA: Mechanics = constrained drag-drop; Dynamics = optimal move discovery; Aesthetic = Mastery + Sensation (satisfying merge animation).
sixDim.who: Free Spirits 40% (experiment, try to break algorithm), Achievers 40% (3-star perfection, Daily leaderboard), Explorers 20% (edge cases in QuickSort pivot)
sixDim.when: Modes unlock sequentially with chapters. Daily Challenge always available. Level Editor from week 2.
sixDim.how: Wrong moves rejected immediately with algorithm-specific explanation ("This violates MergeSort rule: left pointer must advance monotonically"). No ambiguity. 0 false accepts by validation function.
sixDim.where: CSS Grid + HTML5 Drag-and-Drop API, single-file browser app. Level Editor encodes puzzle as shareable URL.

state.narrativeTheme: The SortQuest realm is in chaos — the Library of Ordered Knowledge has been scrambled by the Entropy Demon. As the last Algorithm Keeper, restore order using the ancient sorting laws.
state.characters:
Algorithm Engineer (Student): Selects and deploys correct algorithms to clear each complexity wave using knowledge and pattern recognition.
Complexity Horde (NPC Enemy): Waves of algorithmic problems with specific complexity classes requiring matched algorithm types.
Instructor Avatar (NPC Guide): In-game professor providing contextual hints, chapter intros, and debrief prompts.
state.storyWorld: The Sorting Academy is an ancient institution where computational knowledge is tested not by memorisation but by execution. Six halls represent the six sorting algorithm families: the Bubble Hall, the Selection Chamber, the Insertion Corridor, the Merge Sanctum, the Quick Arena, and the Heap Pinnacle. Each hall tests a specific algorithm family with increasing input complexity and edge-case difficulty. The Academy Boss Puzzle chamber sits at the pinnacle — accessible only to students who have demonstrated mastery across all six halls.

conceptMappings.mechanic: Validation function rejects any move violating algorithm rules
conceptMappings.dynamic: MergeSort: cannot skip merge step; left pointer must advance monotonically
conceptMappings.rationale: Constraint-enforcement turns the puzzle from tile-sort into algorithm-execution: student is constrained to think and act like the algorithm itself

conceptMappings.mechanic: Move counter; star rating penalises excess moves
conceptMappings.dynamic: BubbleSort min 28 moves on 8 tiles; 40 moves = star drops
conceptMappings.rationale: Move counter makes efficiency visceral — fewer moves feels better, which is the intuition behind optimisation

conceptMappings.mechanic: Tap tile to declare pivot; board auto-colours red (>) blue (<); partition follows
conceptMappings.dynamic: Worst-case pivot (always max) → maximally unbalanced → more moves → star drop
conceptMappings.rationale: Visual partition immediately shows why random/median-of-3 strategies outperform worst-case picks

mdaAnalysis.mechanics: Puzzle chain (sequential sorting challenges), Algorithm selection panel, Step-by-step execution validation, Hint token system (3 per session), Sandbox mode (unscored experimentation), Leaderboard by difficulty level reached, Speed-sort challenge mode.
mdaAnalysis.dynamics: The step-by-step execution validation mechanic forces students to think at the algorithm's execution level rather than just knowing the algorithm name, producing deeper cognitive engagement. Hint token scarcity creates authentic cost-benefit reasoning about knowledge confidence. Sandbox mode provides a low-stakes exploration space that complements the scored puzzle chain.
mdaAnalysis.aesthetics: Challenge (each puzzle requires genuine algorithm application, not guessing), Discovery (sandbox mode enables free algorithm experimentation), Accomplishment (advanced difficulty puzzle completion signals genuine SEN4012 mastery), Submission (puzzle aesthetic creates engaging spatial representation of abstract sorting concepts).

state.introText: Welcome to the Sorting Academy. You are an algorithm apprentice, and your task is simple: sort the data correctly, step by step, using the algorithm the puzzle demands. Choose the wrong algorithm and the step-level validator will show exactly where your execution diverged from the correct trace — no vague wrong answers here. Use your hint tokens wisely; you have only three per session. Complete each sorting hall to advance and reach the Boss Puzzle chamber where the full sorting hierarchy is tested in a single complex trial. The Academy is waiting.

preGameSetup.environmentConfig: Arrange seating for individual device use. Confirm browser compatibility on all student devices or lab PCs. Project facilitator dashboard on classroom screen. Test session timer and leaderboard display.
preGameSetup.materialsDistribution: Each student accesses the game via shared URL or QR code. Distribute session handout with game rules summary and XP table. Confirm all students can see the projected leaderboard.
preGameSetup.initialLogEntry: Facilitator logs session start, date, class size, game variant, and initial difficulty setting in the session log.

sessionSteps.activity: Select mode matching chapter. See rules summary + estimated move count for level 1.
Drag tiles; wrong moves rejected with tooltip; correct moves animate. Star rating in real time.
sessionSteps.player: Active tile manipulation; read rejection tooltips

sessionSteps.activity: Global seed — same tiles + mode for all students. Leaderboard by min moves + time.
sessionSteps.facilitator: Post puzzle link to LMS Monday 08:00

sessionSteps.activity: Create custom puzzle; generate shareable URL; share with classmates.

scenarios.name: Campaign

scenarios.setup: Mode + difficulty level selected. Puzzle presented.
scenarios.objective: Solve in minimum moves, under par time, 0 hints
scenarios.winCondition: Solved: min moves, par time, 0 hints
scenarios.storyboard: Mode select → Puzzle presented → Drag attempts → Wrong move rejected + tooltip → Correct sequence → Star rating → Leaderboard
scenarios.outcome: ★★★ (min moves, 0 hints, under par): +100. ★★ (correct, ≤2 hints or >par): +45. ★ (correct, >2 hints): +15.

activities.action: MergeSort mode: merge two sorted columns into one output column
activities.description: Left [2,5,9] + right [1,7,8]. Drag tiles to output following merge rules.
activities.winCondition: Output [1,2,5,7,8,9] via valid merge moves only
activities.winResult: +100 pts (if min + no hints) + MergeMaster badge
activities.loseResult: 0 pts; hint offered free on 4th reset

activities.action: QuickSort mode: select pivot and partition correctly
activities.description: Tap pivot tile. Board auto-colours. Drag partition.
activities.winCondition: Correct pivot; partition in min gestures

scenarios.name: Daily Sort Challenge
scenarios.title: Daily Sort Challenge — Global Leaderboard
scenarios.setup: Global seed published Monday 08:00. All students same puzzle.
scenarios.objective: Solve in min moves within 24h; top leaderboard
scenarios.storyboard: LMS link → Seed loaded → Play (no hints) → Submit → Leaderboard
scenarios.outcome: Top 3: +200 + Daily Champion. Top 10: +100. Correct outside top 10: +50. Incomplete: 0.

activities.action: Complete daily seed in min moves, no hints

scoringSystem.bonuses: ★★★ any puzzle: +5. ★★★ all levels one mode: Mode Master +20. 0-hint session: Zero-Hint badge.
scoringSystem.penalties: Hints: −20 each (never blocked). Excess moves reduce score; completed puzzle minimum 15 pts.
scoringSystem.badges: MergeMaster, Pivot Master, Mode Master, Algorithm Keeper (all 8 modes), Daily Champion, Zero-Hint Scholar, Level Designer

rewardTiers.elite: Algorithm Keeper (★★★ all 8 modes): 5-pt course bonus
rewardTiers.professional: Mode Master (★★★ all levels of 4 modes): 3-pt bonus

tuslerRisks.mitigation: Conduct full platform test on target lab devices 24h before session. Prepare a paper-based fallback version of the sorting puzzle game challenges. Verify browser compatibility on all student devices.
tuslerRisks.responsive: Switch to prepared paper-based fallback immediately. Instructor verbally presents SEN4012 algorithm challenges; students answer on printed worksheets. Session data manually entered into system post-session.

tuslerRisks.risk: Widespread Rule Misunderstanding at Session Start
tuslerRisks.mitigation: Provide multi-modal game introduction: verbal briefing, printed rule card, and in-game tutorial overlay for the sorting puzzle game. Conduct a 2-minute practice round before official scoring begins.
tuslerRisks.responsive: Facilitator pauses game immediately on detecting widespread confusion. Re-delivers rule explanation using a specific worked example. Q&A window opened before resuming. Game timer reset if necessary.

tuslerRisks.mitigation: Design sorting puzzle game scoring to reward both performance and SEN4012 algorithm concept demonstration. Integrate mandatory reflection checkpoints mid-session where students must explain their decision rationale.
tuslerRisks.responsive: Instructor intervenes to redirect student focus to SEN4012 algorithm learning objectives. Post-session assessment weights conceptual understanding over game performance score explicitly.

tuslerRisks.mitigation: Implement adaptive difficulty that reduces challenge intensity if a student is failing repeatedly in the sorting puzzle game. Provide visible progress indicators so students always see incremental advancement.
tuslerRisks.responsive: Facilitator provides individual encouragement and targeted hint. Adaptive system reduces challenge difficulty for the next SEN4012 algorithm task. Optional easier entry point offered without public announcement.

tuslerRisks.risk: Assessment Misalignment with Course Learning Outcomes
tuslerRisks.mitigation: Conduct formal SEN4012 algorithm curriculum alignment review before deployment. Map every sorting puzzle game challenge item to specific course learning outcome statements in the alignment matrix.
tuslerRisks.responsive: Instructor conducts post-session targeted debrief correcting any SEN4012 algorithm misconceptions surfaced by game challenge outcomes. Misaligned challenge items flagged for removal before next session.

tuslerRisks.risk: Incorrect Big-O Reasoning Reinforced as Correct
tuslerRisks.mitigation: Embed automated Big-O correctness checking in the sorting puzzle game — system must validate algorithm classification against verified answer key before awarding XP. Manual review of edge cases by instructor before deployment.
tuslerRisks.responsive: Instructor delivers targeted post-session debrief correcting identified Big-O misconceptions. Specific algorithm challenge items producing widespread incorrect answers flagged for redesign before next session.

tuslerRisks.risk: Step-Level Validation Feels Punitive Rather Than Educational
tuslerRisks.mitigation: Design validation feedback as positive guidance rather than binary fail: 'This step is valid but consider whether this is the most efficient next move.' Animations reinforce correct steps before flagging errors.
tuslerRisks.responsive: Instructor reframes error messages in session debrief as diagnostic information, not punishment. Post-session error pattern analysis shared with students as a personalised learning roadmap for next session.

tuslerRisks.risk: Sandbox Mode Time Consumption Leaving Insufficient Session Time
tuslerRisks.mitigation: Enforce a maximum sandbox time allocation of 8 minutes per session, with a visible countdown and automatic transition to scored puzzle chain when the allocation expires. Sandbox findings prompts students to apply to scored chain.
tuslerRisks.responsive: Facilitator announces sandbox time limit 2 minutes before expiry. Students who have not transitioned receive a facilitator-directed starter puzzle aligned to their sandbox exploration to bridge the transition.

tuslerRisks.risk: Boss Puzzle Algorithm Selection Produces Single Correct Answer Perception
tuslerRisks.mitigation: Design Boss Puzzle with two demonstrably valid algorithm solutions at different complexity-trade-off points. Award full XP for both, but differentiate feedback to highlight the performance difference between the two correct approaches.
tuslerRisks.responsive: Instructor uses Boss Puzzle multiple-valid-solution structure in debrief to teach algorithm selection reasoning beyond binary correctness — reinforcing that SEN4012 algorithm selection is context-dependent, not prescriptive.

Execute sorting rules correctly on given tile arrangement
bloomOutcomes.assessment: ★★★ on ≥4 of 8 modes per student by week 12

bloomOutcomes.outcome: Analyse how move count scales with input size
bloomOutcomes.activity: Free: same mode at level 1 (4 tiles) and level 4 (32 tiles)

kirkpatrick.l1: "How much did SortQuest make sorting intuitive?" (1–5, target ≥4.2)
kirkpatrick.l2: Sorting trace accuracy in timed lab test: ≥75% fully correct (baseline 38%)
kirkpatrick.l3: Correct sorting selection in semester project: ≥70% (baseline 45%)
kirkpatrick.l4: Final exam sorting trace (6 marks): mean ≥4.5/6 (baseline 2.1/6)

kpis.method: Final exam sorting trace (6 marks): mean ≥4.5/6 (baseline 2.1/6)

futureEnhancements: Adaptive Difficulty Engine: Adjust challenge difficulty in real-time based on per-student accuracy history.
AI Hint System: Context-aware Socratic hints triggered after 2 consecutive wrong selections.
Expanded Chapter Coverage: Challenges for all 13 Levitin chapters.
Collaborative Multiplayer Mode: Cooperative challenge mode for paired students.
LMS Grade Passback: Automatic XP-to-grade mapping with Moodle/Canvas gradebook.
state.continuousImprovement: Monthly review of algorithm accuracy; adjust difficulty if <60% first-attempt success. Quarterly correlation with midterm scores; revise concept mapping if r<0.4. Annual Levitin alignment. Peer instructor review before new chapter release.
state.gamePurpose: Make abstract sorting algorithms concrete through physical manipulation with real operation-count cost feedback.
state.gameSubject: Sorting algorithms: bubble, insertion, merge, quick, heap, counting — Levitin Chapters 3-7
state.gameMethod: Flow: puzzle complexity scales with n; students experience O(n²) vs O(n log n) cost difference through XP expenditure.
state.gameMeasurement: Pre/post quiz improvement ≥20% measured 48h after session; L3 transfer assessed via next assignment grade
state.gamePoints: +50 XP for sort completion; bonus XP for fewer-than-optimal moves; leaderboard by total operations
state.gameAchievements: Merge Master: complete merge-sort with 0 extra swaps; Speed Sorter: beat theoretical minimum step count
state.gameBadges: Sort Expert badge: complete all 6 sorting algorithm puzzles; Optimal Sorter: achieve theoretical minimum moves
state.gameOverview: A tile-puzzle visualiser for SEN4012 where students physically sort array tiles using a chosen algorithm — each move costs XP proportional to its operation count.
state.deliverableJust: A browser-based tile puzzle is uniquely suited because: (1) Active decision-making with immediate consequence creates stronger encoding than passive delivery; (2) Game mechanics directly parallel domain processes (see Concept-to-Game Mapping); (3) Performance logging enables objective Bloom L3+ skill assessment.
state.sdtAutonomy: Students choose their approach to each sorting puzzle independently — selecting which sorting algorithm to apply, determining step sequence, and deciding when to use hint tokens versus reasoning independently. The open sandbox mode allows students to freely experiment with algorithm variants without scoring pressure, supporting self-directed algorithm exploration beyond the structured puzzle chain.
state.sdtCompetence: Each puzzle completion with a correct sorting algorithm application awards XP proportional to the solution's time complexity optimality, making algorithm knowledge directly rewarding in a quantifiable way. The puzzle difficulty ladder (Bubble Sort fundamentals to Heap Sort) provides an explicit, topic-aligned competence staircase. Incorrect solutions reveal the execution step where the chosen algorithm diverged from correct behaviour.
state.sdtRelatedness: Post-session puzzle board shared with the class shows which students reached which difficulty levels, creating peer conversation about sorting algorithm trade-offs. Group speed-sort challenges (simultaneous puzzle solving with live class comparison) create shared competitive energy. Pair mode allows two students to collaboratively solve a sorting challenge, requiring negotiated algorithm selection.
state.platform: Browser-based web application; any modern browser. No installation required. Compatible with IKU computer labs, student devices, and mobile browsers.
state.narrativeEvolution:
Hall 1-2 — Bubble and Selection Halls (O(n²) Fundamentals): Apprentice completes Bubble Sort and Selection Sort puzzles at increasing input sizes. Step-level validation reveals common O(n²) execution misconceptions early. Hint tokens tempting but advised to save. SEN4012 concept: O(n²) sorting algorithm execution patterns and comparison count analysis.

Hall 3-4 — Insertion and Merge Halls (Transition to O(n log n)): Insertion Sort puzzles test best-case awareness. Merge Sort introduces divide-and-conquer execution tracing — the first multi-step recursive structure. Complexity jumps; some students use first hint tokens here. SEN4012 concept: insertion sort best-case O(n) and merge sort O(n log n) divide-and-conquer execution.

Hall 5-6 — QuickSort and Heap Halls (O(n log n) Mastery): Pivot selection in QuickSort puzzles tests worst-case awareness. HeapSort introduces heap property validation as a pre-sort step. Advanced students preserve hint tokens; others spend them here. SEN4012 concept: QuickSort pivot strategy, worst-case O(n²) scenario, and HeapSort O(n log n) guarantee.

Boss Puzzle — Mixed-Requirement Trial: The Academy presents a complex scenario with multiple data sets, each requiring a different optimal sorting algorithm. Correct algorithm selection for each data set determines the final quality score. Debrief maps every step-level decision to SEN4012 sorting algorithm selection criteria and comparative analysis.
state.logPrivacy: Activity logs retained 6 months. Accessible only to facilitator and research team. Student identifiers anonymised in aggregate reports. Data per KVKK and GDPR. Students informed via course syllabus. Data destruction certificate at academic year end.
state.howEthics: Student participation is voluntary; equivalent written task available for opt-outs. All performance data anonymised in aggregate reports. Data collected under KVKK and GDPR, disclosed in course syllabus. Assessment based on participation and reflection, not competitive ranking. Instructor monitors emotional wellbeing; distress triggers private check-in.
state.whyMotivation: Students are motivated through three SDT core drives: Autonomy — puzzle approach selection and sandbox exploration give students genuine control over their algorithm learning strategy, respecting that different students prefer different entry points into algorithm understanding. Competence — step-by-step execution validation provides far more precise feedback than answer-level checking, making algorithm understanding feel like a real, verifiable skill rather than an arbitrary test performance. Relatedness — group speed-sort challenges and pair mode transform individual algorithm puzzle-solving into shared class events with authentic peer engagement. Through the Octalysis lens: Challenge, Development & Accomplishment, Discovery, Social Influence.

state.gameIntroScript: Welcome to SortQuest! Your mission: apply the right algorithm to overcome each wave. Every correct selection earns XP. Boss waves test your deepest understanding. Use hint tokens wisely — each costs 5 XP. The Algorithm Master badge awaits students who clear all 12 chapter waves with ≥90% accuracy.

learningContentDetails.teachingSubject: SEN4012: Algorithm complexity analysis, Big-O notation, sorting, searching, dynamic programming, greedy, graph algorithms — Levitin Chapters 2-13
learningContentDetails.declarativeKnowledge: Pre-challenge briefings define algorithm properties and complexity classes using in-game visual explanations

learningContentDetails.methodology: Active learning via consequence-driven decision-making: challenge → decision → immediate feedback → reflection → next challenge

The hardest challenge in a chapter requiring optimal algorithm selection

state.lbCats: Participant Names | Total XP | Badges | Session Rank | Personal Points % = (player XP / total class XP) x 100 | Chapter Completion % | Boss Wave Clears | Accuracy %
state.school: Istanbul Kültür University — Computer Engineering Department
state.instructorRole: The instructor should only be assigned the role of questioning the game. No additional roles beyond questioning should be assigned. The instructor's focus is on facilitating reflection, probing understanding, and guiding critical thinking — not participating in game mechanics. If the instructor participates as a player, grading objectivity and the psychological safety of students may be compromised.
state.gameDurationRules: No single game should exceed 30 minutes of active gameplay. Total session including introduction and debrief: 30 minutes. Recommended timing: Introduction 5 min + Active gameplay 20 min + Debrief & reflection 5 min. Instructor may pause at any time for teaching moments. Games exceeding time limit are concluded at the current phase boundary.

condition1.check: Has the player solved all mandatory puzzles within the time limit?
condition1.outcome: Game ends. Full XP and Escape Artist badge awarded. Debrief commences.

condition2.check: Has the player used all hint tokens without completing the puzzle chain?
condition2.outcome: Game ends. Partial XP for puzzles solved. Instructor walks through solution logic in debrief.

condition3.outcome: Game concludes immediately. XP awarded for puzzles completed. Full solution revealed during debrief.

elements.name: Points & XP
elements.purpose: Quantify learning performance and motivate continued engagement
elements.ontology: earn condition = correct in-game decision or task completion; value = 5-100 XP; tracked per player per session; displayed on dashboard

elements.name: Badges & Achievements
elements.purpose: Reward specific accomplishments beyond cumulative score
elements.ontology: trigger = discrete game event; acquisition = one-time per session; visible on player profile and leaderboard

elements.name: Leaderboard
elements.purpose: Provide social comparison and competitive engagement
elements.ontology: ranking = descending XP order; scope = class-wide session; updated after each phase; anonymised for export

elements.name: Session Timer
elements.purpose: Create urgency and model real-world time constraints
elements.ontology: duration = configured per phase; expiry = triggers phase transition; visible countdown displayed at all times

elements.name: Puzzle Lock System
elements.purpose: Represent domain knowledge as sequential solution gates
elements.ontology: lock = challenge requiring specific knowledge key; key = correct answer; chain = ordered lock sequence to exit

elements.name: Hint Token
elements.purpose: Provide scaffolded support while preserving cognitive effort
elements.ontology: token = limited resource (3 per session); spend = reveals partial solution; tracked per player

elements.name: Room Progression
elements.purpose: Structure exploration with clear spatial and conceptual boundaries
elements.ontology: room = bounded challenge space; unlock = all locks solved; progression = sequential room chain to exit

gameDynamics.progressionSystems: Puzzle chain advances through six sorting algorithm families in complexity order: Bubble Sort, Selection Sort, Insertion Sort, Merge Sort, QuickSort, and Heap Sort. Each family contains 3 puzzles of increasing input size and edge-case complexity. Completing a family unlocks the next and awards a family badge. The final boss puzzle requires choosing and applying the optimal algorithm for a complex mixed-requirement scenario.
gameDynamics.feedbackLoops: Step-validation feedback highlights the exact execution step where a student's algorithm trace diverges from correct behaviour, providing precise actionable feedback. Hint token use is tracked and displayed in the post-session report, providing feedback on knowledge confidence patterns. Sandbox mode provides self-directed feedback through observable algorithm execution without scoring pressure.
gameDynamics.competition: Leaderboard by puzzle chain depth and efficiency (hints used per puzzle). Weekly Sort Master badge for the student with the deepest chain and fewest hints used. Speed-sort challenge mode (all students attempt the same puzzle simultaneously with live class timer) creates high-energy competitive events throughout the semester.
gameDynamics.collaboration: Pair mode pairs students on a shared puzzle: one selects the algorithm, one executes the steps. Role alternation per puzzle ensures both students engage both selection and execution competencies. Disagreements on algorithm selection trigger a collaborative reasoning phase before commitment to the solution path.
gameDynamics.adaptability: Puzzle difficulty and input complexity are configurable per session. Hint token allocation is adjustable for assessment vs practice contexts. Sandbox mode can be enabled or disabled per session. Boss puzzle can be swapped per session to cover different algorithm category combinations. Speed-sort challenge timing is adjustable.

state.personaList:
Persona 1 — The Precision Solver: Approaches each puzzle with full algorithm analysis before beginning. Rarely uses hints. Motivated by completing the boss puzzle with zero hints.

Persona 2 — The Sandbox Explorer: Prefers unscored sandbox mode for algorithm experimentation before attempting scored puzzles. Uses sandbox to test incorrect approaches safely before committing.

Persona 3 — The Speed Runner: Prioritises puzzle completion speed over optimality. May select a correct but suboptimal sorting algorithm for large inputs to avoid decision time cost. Needs instructor prompts to reconsider efficiency.

playerPersonas.name: The Precision Solver
playerPersonas.description: Analyses algorithm suitability fully before beginning execution trace. Motivated by zero-hint boss puzzle completion. Top efficiency leaderboard target.

Achieve optimal algorithm selection for boss puzzle mixed-requirement scenario

playerPersonas.name: The Sandbox Explorer
playerPersonas.description: Uses sandbox mode as primary learning space before attempting scored chain. Algorithm understanding developed through experimental execution rather than prior theory.
motivations: Discover all sorting algorithm execution patterns through sandbox exploration
Complete at least three puzzle chain families per session
Use sandbox insights to achieve correct boss puzzle algorithm selection
challenges: Sandbox time allocation leaves insufficient time for full scored chain
Needs structured transition signal from sandbox to scored chain

rewardStructure.elite: Sort Master badge + 'Sorting Academy Graduate' certificate for completing the full puzzle chain including Boss Puzzle with zero hints used
rewardStructure.professional: Algorithm Scholar badge + leaderboard recognition for completing ≥4 sorting algorithm families with ≤1 hint token used per family
rewardStructure.access: Participation certificate + 'Sorting Apprentice' badge for correctly completing ≥8 sorting algorithm puzzles across any difficulty tiers

intrinsic: Precise satisfaction of correctly tracing algorithm execution step-by-step — a level of mastery that abstract answers cannot provide
Discovery of sorting algorithm execution characteristics through sandbox experimentation that deepens conceptual understanding
Social recognition from class speed-sort competitions and efficiency leaderboard comparison across sessions

teamBased.trigger: Class-wide session highest aggregate puzzle depth reached
Pair mode duo completing Boss Puzzle with zero hints in minimum time

individual.trigger: Completing full puzzle chain including Boss Puzzle with zero hint tokens used
Completing all available sorting algorithm explorations in sandbox mode
Solving Boss Puzzle on first attempt with optimal algorithm selection

socialInteraction.collaboration: Pair mode assigns algorithm selection to one student and execution trace to the other — creating complementary collaboration that ensures both students engage both cognitive layers of sorting algorithm mastery.
socialInteraction.competition: Class speed-sort challenge mode creates simultaneous competitive puzzle solving visible to all students — turning individual algorithm practice into a shared competitive performance event.
socialInteraction.communityBuilding: Post-session class puzzle board showing depth reached and hints used creates transparent peer comparison that drives improvement across sessions; sandbox findings are shared in class debrief.

conceptualMap.description: Six-family puzzle chain maps directly to the SEN4012 sorting algorithm taxonomy — Bubble, Selection, Insertion, Merge, Quick, Heap — in complexity progression order.
conceptualMap.courseReference: Cormen et al. (2022): Chapters 6-9 — Sorting Algorithms

conceptualMap.description: Step validation forces students to trace algorithm execution precisely — developing execution-level understanding that distinguishes genuine algorithm knowledge from name recognition.
conceptualMap.courseReference: Cormen et al. (2022): Chapter 2 — Insertion Sort and Loop Invariants

conceptualMap.description: Boss puzzle presents scenarios requiring selection of the optimal algorithm across multiple competing criteria — operationalising the SEN4012 algorithm selection decision framework.
Sandbox mode operationalises algorithmic hypothesis testing — students can experiment with algorithm variants on test inputs without scoring pressure, developing genuine algorithm intuition.

Algorithm selection panel presents competing sorting options with different complexity profiles — requiring students to reason about best-case, worst-case, and average-case performance simultaneously.
conceptualMap.courseReference: Cormen et al. (2022): Chapter 8 — Linear-Time Sorting

display: Personal XP % = (player XP / total class XP) × 100
leaderboardDetails.personalFormula: Personal XP % = (player XP / total class XP) × 100
classWideMetrics: Class average Sorting accuracy per session
leaderboardDetails.recognition: Session leaders earn bonus XP and leaderboard recognition
leaderboardDetails.updateFrequency: Real-time update after each phase; final standing after session end

continuousImprovements.name: Adaptive Complexity Scaling
continuousImprovements.description: Implement algorithm-driven difficulty adjustment that scales sorting algorithm challenge complexity per student based on real-time session performance, ensuring optimal challenge across all SEN4012 skill levels.

continuousImprovements.name: Big-O Ontology Integration
continuousImprovements.description: Extend the puzzle game challenge pool using a formal Big-O complexity ontology, enabling systematic verification that all SEN4012 algorithm topics are covered proportionally across sessions.

continuousImprovements.name: LLM Hint Engine
continuousImprovements.description: Integrate an LLM-powered hint system that generates Socratic prompts aligned to SEN4012 Big-O analysis methodology, reducing facilitator dependency for in-session algorithm guidance.

continuousImprovements.name: Cross-Session Mastery Tracking
continuousImprovements.description: Implement longitudinal tracking of sorting algorithm algorithm knowledge across multiple sessions to identify persistent misconceptions and generate targeted SEN4012 revision recommendations.

continuousImprovements.name: Assessment Export Module
continuousImprovements.description: Add certified assessment mode exporting individual Big-O knowledge profiles as grade-ready reports aligned to SEN4012 course learning outcomes rubric.
